macro_segments.title: Introduction to the Laws of Worker's Right to Eat and Muzzling the Ox

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks and context of the daf
Torah sources for worker rights and prohibition of muzzling
Ravina's position: no gezerah shavah needed; direct derivation from lo tachsom shor

macro_segments.title: Gezerah Shavah from Shabbat to Expand the Scope of Lo Tachsom Shor

micro_segments.title: The gezerah shavah of 'shor' from Shabbat: all animals, not just oxen
Why the Torah mentions an ox specifically: to equate muzzler and muzzled
Logical equivalence: attached produce for human and animal workers

macro_segments.title: Tannaitic Baraitot on Threshing: What Produce and What Stage

micro_segments.title: First baraita: dayish applies only to things that grow from the ground
Exclusion of milking, curdling, and cheese-making (non-ground-grown work)
Gemara challenges: why learn from dayish when kerem re'cha already teaches this?
Response: repetition of dayish limits to things that grow from the ground

macro_segments.title: Second Baraita on Dayish: The Time Dimension (Gmar Melacha)

micro_segments.title: Dayish is distinct in that work occurs at the time of gmar melacha
Exclusion: weeding of garlic and onions (no gmar melacha)
Gemara challenges: why learn from dayish when el kelycha lo titein teaches gmar melacha?
Refinement: plucking small onions among large ones (not gmar melacha of the small ones)

macro_segments.title: Third Baraita: Gmar Melacha for Ma'aser versus Challa

micro_segments.title: Dayish: gmar melacha has not occurred for ma'aser yet; worker still eats
Exclusion: separating pressed dates and figs (already gmar melacha for ma'aser)
Rav Papa's resolution: unripe dates stored for ripening (not yet gmar melacha)

macro_segments.title: Fourth Baraita: Gmar Melacha for Challa Extends Worker's Rights

micro_segments.title: Dayish: gmar melacha has not occurred for challa; worker eats
Exclusion: kneading, slapping, baking (gmar melacha for challa)
Apparent contradiction: grain already obligated in ma'aser, yet worker eats until challa

macro_segments.title: Resolution of Ma'aser vs. Challa Contradiction

micro_segments.title: Abaye's solution: case is outside Eretz Yisrael where ma'aser doesn't apply
Gemara's objection: challa and ma'aser are equally affected by location
Alternative: seven years of conquest and seven years of division (historical case)
Ravina's synthesis: combine both baraitot to include both ma'aser and challa
Subsequent interpretations: Raavad (ma'aser takes precedence) vs. Rashi/Tosafot (context-dependent)

macro_segments.title: Question: Can a Worker Char or Grill Grain Before Eating?

micro_segments.title: Issue posed: is charring grain in fire like davar acher (additives)?
Baraita evidence: owner can give wine to workers; workers can dip bread in brine
Gemara's distinction: concern is not making person more fit, but altering the produce

macro_segments.title: Baraita on Eating at Head of Rows: Timing and Preparation Restrictions

micro_segments.title: Workers eat grapes at the head of rows, but cannot char them in fire
Gemara's explanation: concern is bitur melacha (wasting owner's time)
Hypothetical case: if wife and children prepare while worker continues, does charring become permitted?

macro_segments.title: Baraita on Prohibited Preparations: Prohibitions on Dipping and Breaking Produce

micro_segments.title: Workers cannot char in fire, bury in warm earth, or break on rock
Permitted: crushing with hand as part of eating process
Gemara questions: are these prohibitions about davar acher or bitur melacha?
Gemara argues: rock-breaking doesn't obviously sweeten or affect taste

macro_segments.title: Comprehensive Baraita on Worker Rights: Times and Conditions of Eating

micro_segments.title: Workers harvesting figs, dates, grapes, and olives are permitted to eat and exempt from terumah and ma'aser
Workers cannot eat with bread unless owner gives permission
Workers cannot eat with salt unless owner gives permission

macro_segments.title: Comparison Baraita: Paid Worker vs. Torah-Granted Rights

micro_segments.title: Worker hired for non-harvest work (tilling, circling) cannot eat
Worker hired for harvest (letzor, limsok, lilkot) can eat and is exempt
If worker made deal with owner to gain eating rights: can eat one by one, not two by two
When eating with salt after making paid deal: one by one permitted, two by two prohibited

macro_segments.title: Gemara's Analysis: Which Baraita Case? Distinguishing Scenarios

micro_segments.title: If latter baraita refers to paid deal, all eating methods permitted without restriction
Therefore latter baraita must refer to Torah-granted rights, yet permits salt
Abaye's solution: distinction between Eretz Yisrael and Chutz L'aretz regarding tfisah (acquisition)
Rava's objection: if ma'aser creates tfisah in Eretz Yisrael, so would salt; location distinction doesn't work

macro_segments.title: Rava's Resolution: Two Independent Factors for Tfisah

micro_segments.title: Factor 1: Quantity—one fruit doesn't obligate in terumah/ma'aser; two does
Factor 2: Purchase deal with owner—obligates independently of salt or quantity
Synthesis: two scenarios create tfisah—purchase (any quantity) or salt with two fruits
Normal worker (no purchase, no salt): can eat two by two
Normal worker with salt: only one by one (dipping plus quantity makes tfisah)
Even with owner's permission: salt plus two creates obligation in terumah/ma'aser

macro_segments.title: Rav Matna's Tannaitic Source: The Goren Concept and Two-Fruit Threshold

micro_segments.title: Scriptural basis: 'ki kavtzam ka-amir gorna' (gathered like sheaf into granary)
Goren as conceptual stage where obligation begins; requires gathering of multiple items
Two items represent minimum threshold for gathering; one item insufficient

topical_tags.term: el kelycha lo titein (don't put in vessel)
terumah and ma'aser (priestly gifts and tithes)
Eretz Yisrael vs. Chutz L'aretz (Land of Israel vs. Diaspora)
gezeirah shavah from Shabbat (shor, l'ma'an yanuach)
